questions.question: Which is a complete sentence?
options: She runs through the park daily.
questions.explanation: D has subject (She) + verb (runs) + complete thought ✅

questions.question: What type of sentence is this? 'I wanted to stay, but I had to leave.'
questions.explanation: Two simple sentences joined by 'but' (FANBOYS) = Compound

questions.question: Identify the run-on:
options: She was tired. She went to bed.
She was tired, so she went to bed.
She was tired she went to bed.
Although she was tired, she went to bed.
questions.explanation: C is a fused sentence (no punctuation between two complete sentences)

questions.question: 'Although it was raining ___' This is a:
questions.explanation: 'Although it was raining' is a dependent clause - needs a main clause to complete it.

questions.question: Which correctly fixes this comma splice? 'She was sad, she cried all day.'
options: She was sad she cried all day.
She was sad, and she cried all day.
She was sad, but she cried all day.
She was sad, yet she cried all day.
questions.explanation: Add FANBOYS after comma. 'and' makes logical sense here.

questions.question: Which is a COMPLEX sentence?
questions.explanation: C has a subordinating conjunction 'because' making it complex.

questions.question: 'When the match ended ___' To complete this sentence correctly:
options: When the match ended, we celebrated.
When the match ended. We celebrated.
When the match ended we celebrating.
When the match ended but we celebrated.
questions.explanation: Dependent clause first → needs comma then main clause.

questions.question: What does a compound-complex sentence have?
options: Two main clauses and one dependent clause
One main clause and two dependent clauses

questions.question: Fix the fragment: 'The old book with a torn cover on the dusty shelf.'
options: The old book with a torn cover was on the dusty shelf.
On the dusty shelf the old book.
Because the old book with a torn cover.
questions.explanation: Added verb 'was' to make it a complete sentence with subject + verb + complete thought.
